Healthcare WPV: why a plain Y/N isn't the whole story
Enacted and in force (or signed, phasing in)
Pending — introduced / in committee / passed one chamber; not yet law
Bill failed at end of session (sine die)
Passed the legislature but governor vetoed — not law
Covered indirectly (e.g., CA retail via general-industry SB553)
Ambiguous / transitional status (e.g., near-final but unsigned)
No prevention-program/device mandate, but a criminal penalty-enhancement law exists. Counts as N for purchase-driving purposes

A bill is pending but nothing is in force yet (e.g., Wyoming)
Enacted but only requires incident reporting, not a full program/device
Applies to PUBLIC employers only, not private
Enacted but only 'may' / 'must consider' — no install mandate
The statute's actual verb — permissive, not mandatory
A facility-MAPPING mandate, not a panic-alert (Alyssa's) law
A funded state program/initiative, not a statutory mandate
Scope / notability tags on an enacted mandate
Enacted at CITY level only — no statewide law
Enacted statewide with a room-count threshold / bill cite
Cross-sector, ALL-employer WPV mandate (e.g., CA SB553). 'N' = no all-employer mandate (state may still have industry-specific ones)
GOLD = Y enacted + mandatory + dated + applies to your buyer. Score down anything with a softening qualifier (penalty/permissive/public/reporting/mapping/local/~) or P / Dead / Vetoed
